content.body: Reproduction is the production of new individuals. Asexual reproduction uses one parent; sexual reproduction uses two parents. Both have advantages and disadvantages.

Asexual reproduction:
One parent, no gametes, offspring genetically identical (clones)
Methods: binary fission (bacteria), budding (yeast/Hydra), spore formation (fungi/ferns), vegetative propagation (plants), regeneration

Sexual reproduction:
Two parents, gametes fuse (fertilisation), genetic variation in offspring

content.title: Asexual Methods
content.body:
Binary fission: single cell divides into two identical cells (bacteria divide every 20 min)
Budding: new organism grows from parent (yeast)
Vegetative propagation: stems, roots, leaves form new plants (potato tubers, strawberry runners, Bryophyllum leaf notches)
Spores: lightweight, many produced, dispersed by wind

content.title: Advantages Compared
content.body:
Asexual advantages: fast, one parent needed, all offspring survive if conditions good
Asexual disadvantages: no variation, vulnerability to disease
Sexual advantages: variation → adaptation → evolution
Sexual disadvantages: slower, need two parents, gamete production energy costly

questions.question: Asexual reproduction produces offspring that are
questions.answer: genetically identical to parent (clones)
questions.explanation: Asexual: one parent, mitosis only → clones with identical DNA

questions.question: Bacteria reproduce by
questions.answer: binary fission
questions.explanation: Bacteria split into two equal halves: binary fission. Can double every 20 minutes

questions.question: Strawberry plants spread vegetatively using
questions.answer: runners (horizontal stems)
questions.explanation: Strawberry stolons (runners) grow horizontally and produce new plants at nodes

questions.question: Main advantage of sexual reproduction over asexual is
options: genetic variation enabling adaptation to changing environments
questions.answer: genetic variation enabling adaptation to changing environments
questions.explanation: Sexual reproduction shuffles genes producing variation — raw material for natural selection and evolution

questions.question: Spores as a reproductive method are advantageous because
options: they are produced in small numbers
they are lightweight and produced in huge numbers for dispersal
questions.answer: they are lightweight and produced in huge numbers for dispersal
questions.explanation: Spores: small, light, produced by millions, easily wind-dispersed to new locations

questions.question: Which is NOT asexual reproduction?
questions.answer: fertilisation of egg by sperm
questions.explanation: Fertilisation is the defining event of sexual reproduction

questions.question: Potato tubers are used for vegetative propagation because
options: they are stem structures with buds (eyes) that grow into new plants
questions.answer: they are stem structures with buds (eyes) that grow into new plants
questions.explanation: Potato tuber is a modified stem with axillary buds (eyes) that sprout into new plants

questions.question: Yeast reproduces asexually by
questions.answer: budding
questions.explanation: Yeast: small bud grows from parent cell, eventually pinches off as separate organism

questions.question: A disease wipes out an asexually reproducing population because
options: all individuals are genetically identical so no individual has resistance
questions.answer: all individuals are genetically identical so no individual has resistance
questions.explanation: Clonal populations: if one is susceptible to a pathogen, ALL are susceptible — no resistant variants

questions.question: Bryophyllum reproduces vegetatively via
questions.answer: leaf notches producing plantlets
questions.explanation: Bryophyllum (Kalanchoe) produces miniature plantlets along leaf margins — these fall and grow into new plants

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: The human reproductive system is specialised for internal fertilisation and development of offspring within the mother.

Male reproductive system:
Testes: produce sperm (spermatogenesis) and testosterone
Epididymis: sperm maturation and storage
Vas deferens: sperm transport tube
Seminal vesicles, prostate: produce seminal fluid
Urethra: carries semen and urine (not simultaneously)

Female reproductive system:
Ovaries: produce eggs (oogenesis) and hormones (oestrogen, progesterone)
Oviduct (Fallopian tube): egg transport, fertilisation site
Uterus (womb): embryo implants and develops
Cervix: lower uterus
Vagina: birth canal

content.title: The Menstrual Cycle
content.body:
28-day cycle (average):
Day 1-5: Menstruation (uterus lining shed)
Day 1-13: Follicular phase — FSH stimulates follicle development, oestrogen rises
Day 14: Ovulation — LH surge triggers egg release
Day 15-28: Luteal phase — corpus luteum produces progesterone, thickens uterus lining
If no fertilisation: progesterone falls → menstruation

content.title: Fertilisation and Implantation
content.body:
Fertilisation: sperm meets egg in oviduct
Zygote → divides → blastocyst → implants in uterus wall
Placenta forms: exchanges nutrients, O₂, waste between mother and foetus
Amnion: fluid-filled sac protecting embryo

questions.question: Sperm are produced in the
questions.answer: seminiferous tubules of testes
questions.explanation: Spermatogenesis occurs in the seminiferous tubules within the testes

questions.question: Fertilisation normally occurs in the
questions.answer: oviduct (Fallopian tube)
questions.explanation: Egg released from ovary travels along oviduct where it may be fertilised by sperm

questions.question: During the menstrual cycle, ovulation occurs at approximately day
questions.explanation: Ovulation typically occurs at day 14 of a 28-day cycle, triggered by LH surge

questions.question: The corpus luteum produces
questions.answer: progesterone (to maintain uterus lining)
questions.explanation: After ovulation, the corpus luteum secretes progesterone keeping the uterus lining thick for implantation

questions.question: The placenta's function is to
options: exchange nutrients, O₂ and CO₂ between mother and foetus
questions.answer: exchange nutrients, O₂ and CO₂ between mother and foetus
questions.explanation: Placenta: maternal and foetal blood flow close together — substances diffuse across without mixing

questions.question: If fertilisation does not occur, menstruation happens because
options: progesterone level falls, uterus lining breaks down
questions.answer: progesterone level falls, uterus lining breaks down
questions.explanation: Without implantation, corpus luteum degenerates, progesterone falls → uterus lining shed

questions.question: The amniotic fluid functions to
options: cushion and protect the embryo from shock
questions.answer: cushion and protect the embryo from shock
questions.explanation: Amniotic fluid: shock absorber, maintains temperature, allows foetal movement

questions.question: FSH is produced by the
questions.answer: pituitary gland
questions.explanation: FSH (Follicle Stimulating Hormone) is secreted by anterior pituitary and stimulates follicle development

questions.question: A typical human pregnancy lasts approximately
questions.answer: 9 months (38-40 weeks)
questions.explanation: Human gestation is ~40 weeks (38 weeks from fertilisation) from last menstrual period

questions.question: Oestrogen in the menstrual cycle is responsible for
options: thickening uterus lining and triggering LH surge
questions.answer: thickening uterus lining and triggering LH surge
questions.explanation: Rising oestrogen in follicular phase rebuilds uterus lining and at peak triggers LH surge → ovulation

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Reproduction. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Reproduction concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Reproduction builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 3.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Reproduction. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 3.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Reproduction. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Reproduction include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Reproduction. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Reproduction. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Reproduction concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Reproduction builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Reproduction. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Reproduction. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Reproduction include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Reproduction. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.answer: The cell
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.answer: Mitochondria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.answer: DNA (Deoxyribonucleic acid)
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.answer: Site of photosynthesis
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.answer: Red blood cells (haemoglobin)
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.answer: Kidneys
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.answer: Gas exchange and transpiration
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.answer: Axon
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.answer: Carbon dioxide (CO₂)
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).
